mnemonics: FINite COVERage For Any Tiny Epsilon (FINCOVATE): A set is totally bounded if, for any given tiny distance (epsilon), you can cover it with a FINite number of small sets (epsilon-balls).
keyPoints: A set is totally bounded if for every ε > 0, it can be covered by finitely many balls of radius ε.
Total boundedness is a 'finiteness' condition related to the space's 'size' or 'diameter'.
In a complete metric space, a set is compact if and only if it is closed and totally bounded.
commonMistakes: Confusing 'totally bounded' with 'bounded'. Every totally bounded set is bounded, but not every bounded set is totally bounded (e.g., the unit ball in an infinite-dimensional space).
Thinking the centers of the epsilon-balls must be inside the set A. They can be anywhere in the larger metric space X.
